paper_title: Haslea Culture, Photosynthesis Measurement, and Analysis

methods.description: Procedure for isolating, culturing, and monitoring the growth of Haslea ostrearia and Haslea silbo strains under controlled laboratory conditions.

steps.description: Two strains of H. ostrearia were isolated from the west coast of France at the Île de Ré and Baie de Bourgneuf in 2015. A single strain of H. silbo was isolated from North Carolina, USA, also in 2015.
steps.searchable_summary: isolate algal strains from specified locations
inputs: environmental samples from Île de Ré
environmental samples from Baie de Bourgneuf

steps.description: Cells were collected by micropipette.
steps.searchable_summary: collect individual cells using micropipette

steps.description: Cells were grown in f/2 medium (Guillard and Ryther 1962).
steps.searchable_summary: culture cells in f/2 medium

steps.description: Growth and successful selection of H. ostrearia were monitored by light microscopy.
steps.searchable_summary: monitor culture growth and purity using light microscopy

steps.description: Once sufficient single species biomass had been obtained, cultures were transferred to the laboratory at Mount Allison University, Sackville, Canada.
steps.searchable_summary: transfer cultures to laboratory

steps.description: Cultures were grown in f/2 medium (Guillard and Ryther 1962) at 20 °C and at a light intensity of 50 µmol photons m −2 s −1 , with a light/dark cycle of 12 h:12 h.
steps.searchable_summary: grow cultures under controlled temperature, light intensity, and light/dark cycle
inputs: 50 µmol photons m−2 s−1 light intensity

steps.description: Growth of cultures was monitored by following fluorescence emission at 680 nm using a plate spectrofluorometer (SpectraMax Gemini EM, Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, USA).
steps.searchable_summary: monitor culture growth by measuring fluorescence emission at 680 nm using a plate spectrofluorometer

methods.method_name: Rapid Light Curve (RLC) Measurement using FRRf
methods.description: Procedure for measuring chlorophyll fluorescence induction curves using Fast Repetition Rate fluorescence (FRRf) under a series of changing light levels to generate non-sequential Rapid Light Curves (RLCs).

steps.description: When cultures reached mid-exponential phase, culture subsamples were taken for chlorophyll fluorescence induction measurements.
steps.searchable_summary: take culture subsamples at mid-exponential growth phase

steps.description: Subsamples were placed in a 2 ml cuvette.
steps.searchable_summary: transfer subsamples to a 2 ml cuvette

steps.description: Samples were then dark-adapted for ~ 2 min.
steps.searchable_summary: dark-adapt samples for approximately 2 minutes

steps.description: Samples were then exposed to a series of 30 s exposures to changing light levels (Fig. 1) to result in a non-sequential rapid light curve (RLC).
steps.searchable_summary: expose samples to a series of changing light levels for 30 seconds each to generate an RLC

steps.description: At the end of each 30 s exposure we applied a train of 40 blue (455 nm) flashlets with a duration of 1.2 µs each separated by an intervening interval of 1.0 µs of darkness to induce an FRRf fluorescence induction curve (Kolber et al. 1998), using a Photon Systems Instruments FL3500 fluorometer system (Brno, Czech Republic).
steps.searchable_summary: apply FRRf flash sequence (40 blue flashlets, 1.2 µs duration, 1.0 µs dark interval) at the end of each light exposure using an FRRf fluorometer

steps.description: Flashlet intensity was selected to saturate the fluorescence rise within around 30 of 40 flashlets (Laney 2003; Laney and Letelier 2008).
steps.searchable_summary: calibrate flashlet intensity to saturate fluorescence rise within a specified number of flashlets

methods.description: Procedure for analyzing the raw FRRf fluorescence induction curve data to fit a biophysical model and extract key photosynthetic parameters.

steps.description: For each FRRf induction curve, data were exported from the FluorWin data capture software.
steps.searchable_summary: export FRRf induction curve data from data acquisition software

steps.description: Fit a model with four parameters: minimal fluorescence, F 0 ; maximal fluorescence, F M ; effective absorption cross section for PSII photochemistry, σ PSII ; coefficient of excitonic connectivity ρ; and the rapid and slow kinetic phase lifetimes for PSII reopening, τ 1 and τ 2 , respectively (Kolber et al. 1998) using the PSIWORX-R package (A. Barnett, sourceforge.net) Ni et al. 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: fit FRRf induction curve data to a biophysical model (Kolber et al. 1998) using PSIWORX-R package to estimate F0, FM, sigmaPSII, rho, tau1, tau2
outputs: estimated parameters (F0, FM, sigmaPSII, rho, tau1, tau2)

steps.description: For each measurement we applied an FRRf induction before and then again after a 1 s period of darkness to allow PSII to reopen after illumination.
steps.searchable_summary: perform FRRf measurements before and after a 1-second dark period

steps.description: We thus determined in the dark F 0 , F M , σ PSII , ρ; under actinic light F S , F M ʹ, σ PSII ʹ ρʹ; and following 1 s of darkness after actinic light F 0 ʹ1s, F M ʹ1s, ρʹ1s, σ PSII ʹ1s.
steps.searchable_summary: determine FRRf parameters (F0, FM, sigmaPSII, rho, FS, FM', sigmaPSII', rho', F0'1s, FM'1s, rho'1s, sigmaPSII'1s) under different light conditions (dark, actinic light, 1s dark after light)
inputs: measurement conditions (dark, light, 1s dark after light)
outputs: FRRf parameters specific to light conditions

methods.description: Procedure for calculating derived photosynthetic parameters such as F0', qP, Y(NPQ), and ETR from the measured FRRf parameters.

steps.description: We then estimated F 0 ʹ as: 1s (Oxborough and Baker 1997).
steps.searchable_summary: estimate F0' using F0'1s based on Oxborough and Baker (1997)

steps.description: We estimated the coefficient of photochemical quenching (q P ): Kramer et al. 2004).
steps.searchable_summary: estimate coefficient of photochemical quenching (qP) using formula from Kramer et al. (2004)

steps.description: We estimated the yield of non-photochemical quenching.
steps.searchable_summary: estimate yield of non-photochemical quenching (Y(NPQ))

steps.description: For plotting the non-sequential light curves, we calculated the ETR as: where Y(PSII) is the quantum efficiency of PSII calculated as (F M ʹ-F S )/F M ʹ. This is modified from Suggett et al. (2009) as we did not include n PSII (usually set to 0.002) and we expressed ETR as e− PSII −1 s −1 .
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Electron Transport Rate (ETR) for RLCs using Y(PSII) and sigmaPSII', modified from Suggett et al. (2009)

methods.description: Procedure for inhibiting regulated non-photochemical quenching (NPQ) using DL-dithiothreitol (DTT) and subsequently measuring Rapid Light Curves (RLCs).

steps.description: DTT was prepared as fresh 5 mM stock on each day of measurements dissolved in ethanol.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare fresh 5 mM DTT stock solution in ethanol daily

steps.description: 20 µL aliquots of the stock were added to 2 ml of culture to produce a final concentration of 5 µM which was reported to fully inhibit Stern-Volmer non-photochemical quenching (NPQ).
steps.searchable_summary: add DTT stock to culture subsamples to achieve a final concentration of 5 µM
outputs: DTT-treated culture subsamples (5 µM final concentration)

steps.description: These treatments were then examined using the same non-sequential RLC as described above, with five replicate RLCs for each Haslea strain.
steps.searchable_summary: perform non-sequential RLC measurements on DTT-treated culture subsamples

methods.description: Procedure for exposing Haslea cultures to light stress followed by dark recovery, measuring chlorophyll fluorescence using FRRf throughout the process.

steps.description: Subsamples from each culture, as described above were exposed to actinic light (induction phase) prior to a dark recovery period.
steps.searchable_summary: take culture subsamples for induction-recovery experiment

steps.description: Two light levels were used, 300 and 600 µmol photons m −2 s −1 to represent comparatively lower and higher light stress.
steps.searchable_summary: select two actinic light levels (300 and 600 µmol photons m−2 s−1) for induction phase

steps.description: The induction phase lasted 1200 s (20 min) and was followed by a dark recovery phase of 900 s (15 min).
steps.searchable_summary: apply actinic light for 1200 seconds (induction) followed by 900 seconds of darkness (recovery)

steps.description: Measurements were made using the FRRf fluorometer and data analysis procedures as described for the RLCs described above.
steps.searchable_summary: perform FRRf measurements and data analysis (Methods 2, 3, 4) throughout the induction and recovery phases
outputs: FRRf data and calculated parameters over time

methods.description: General procedures for performing statistical analysis and plotting of the experimental data.

steps.description: Statistical analyses and plotting were performed using R Version 3.3.2 (R Core Team 2016) with the package 'minpack.lm ' (Timur et al. 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: perform statistical analyses and plotting using R with the 'minpack.lm' package
